title: Ovarian Torsion
oneLiner: Gynaecological emergency where ovary twists on pedicle causing ischaemia; rapid diagnosis (hours) and surgery needed to preserve ovary and fertility.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** — *Finding: positive B-hCG + haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension, peritonism).* Haemorrhagic shock kills faster than torsion.
- **Tubo-ovarian abscess / septic shock** — *Finding: high fever (>38.5°C) + purulent discharge + cervical motion tenderness + leukocytosis (WCC >15).* Sepsis can mimic torsion but needs antibiotics > surgery initially.
- **Ovarian torsion with complete infarction and peritonitis** — *Finding: diffuse guarding/rigidity + rising lactate (>2.5) + NEWS2 ≥5 with no improvement.* Can progress to multi-organ failure if surgery delayed.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms** (3–5 things you’ll actually see):
    - Sudden-onset severe unilateral **lower abdominal / pelvic pain** (often colicky, may wax/ wane if intermittent torsion)
    - Pain **aggravated by movement** (coughing, walking)
    - **Nausea and/or vomiting** (common, can be prominent)
    - **Dysuria** or urinary frequency (due to bladder irritation)
    - **Fever** (late sign, suggests necrosis or superinfection)
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Unilateral **adnexal tenderness** on bimanual exam (bimanual is essential)
    - Lower abdominal tenderness ± **guarding/rebound** (peritoneal signs indicate advanced ischaemia)
    - Palpable **adnexal mass** (if >5 cm; often cystic or solid)
    - **Cervical motion tenderness** (non-specific)
    - Vital signs: tachycardia (early peritonism), hypotension (late, suspect rupture or septic shock)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely → less likely with 1-line differentiators:
    - **Ectopic pregnancy:** positive B-hCG + free fluid on bedside US + cervical motion tenderness
    - **Ruptured ovarian cyst:** sudden pain but often with **transient hypotension** and free fluid; ultrasound shows collapsed cyst
    - **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID):** bilateral pain, vaginal discharge, fever, raised inflammatory markers; **cervical motion tenderness** on bimanual
    - **Appendicitis:** pain starts periumbilical then localises to RIF, anorexia, vomiting; **McBurney’s sign**, not adnexal tenderness
    - **Renal colic:** flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, costovertebral angle tenderness
    - **Diverticulitis:** left lower quadrant pain, fever, diarrhoea or constipation; **left-sided tenderness**, no adnexal mass
    - **Mesenteric adenitis:** right-sided pain + fever, but **lymphadenopathy** on US, and often normal gynaecological exam
- **Splitter:** *Positive pregnancy test + unilateral adnexal pain = consider ectopic first, not torsion. Order B-hCG stat.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
    - Urine **pregnancy test** (mandatory in any reproductive-age woman with lower abdominal pain)
    - **Vital signs** (tachycardia → hypovolaemia from rupture? hypotension → catastrophic)
    - **Urine dip** (rule out UTI/renal colic)
- **Bloods:**
    - **FBC** (WCC rise – infection/infarction; Hb drop – haemorrhage)
    - **CRP** (high in PID, infection, late torsion)
    - **Beta-hCG** (serum if urine positive or uncertain)
    - **U&Es, LFTs** (pre-operative baseline)
    - **Lactate** (elevated if ischaemic, can help confirm urgency)
    - **Group & save** (prepare for possible blood products)
    - **Coagulation screen** (pre-operative)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** **Transvaginal ultrasound with colour Doppler** (urgent, if available) – look for:
        - Enlarged ovary (≥4 cm), hyperechoic stroma, **whirlpool sign** of twisted vascular pedicle
        - **Absent or reduced arterial flow** on Doppler (not 100% sensitive; intermittent torsion may show flow)
        - Free fluid in pouch of Douglas (suggests rupture or ischaemia)
    - **Second-line:** **MRI abdomen** if US equivocal and still high suspicion (shows associated adnexal mass, twisted pedicle) – but **do not wait for MRI if classical presentation**
- **Special tests:** None routine; **diagnostic laparoscopy** is both gold standard and definitive treatment
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine B-hCG** – omission = potential missed ectopic with catastrophic haemorrhage
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **Stabilise** – IV access (two wide-bore cannulae if shock), fluid bolus (Hartmann’s 500 mL stat, repeat as needed)
    - **Analgesia** – morphine 2.5–5 mg IV every 4–6 hours PRN (with antiemetic)
    - **Antiemetic** – ondansetron 4 mg IV slow bolus
    - **NBM** (NBM from time of suspicion – for potential urgent laparoscopy)
    - **Urgent gynaecology referral** (on-call registrar; discuss within 15–30 minutes)
    - **Pregnancy test result** – if positive, urgent gynaecology + consider ectopic pathway
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - **Pre-operative:** monitor vital signs (NEWS2), repeat lactate if deteriorating; keep NBM; IV fluids at maintenance (Hartmann’s 1 L/8h [check local policy])
    - **Post-operative:**
        - Oral intake commenced once awake (usually day 1)
        - Analgesia (paracetamol 1 g QDS, tramadol [check local] or morphine PRN)
        - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily if BMI <30, higher if obese – but not if high bleeding risk)
        - Wound care – early mobilisation, encourage regular observations for signs of infection
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - If **ovarian cyst** detected (common cause), arrange outpatient ultrasound follow-up in 6 weeks to monitor cyst resolution
    - **Fertility follow-up:** if only one ovary preserved, reassure re fertility but discuss future pregnancy risks (adhesions, torsion of contralateral ovary)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **IV fluids:** Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline at 1 mL/kg/h (adjusted for NBM)
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if not high bleed risk)
    - **Diet:** NBM until seen by anaesthetist/gynaecologist (if pre-op); clear fluids post-op if tolerating
    - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly, escalate if ≥5
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Analgesia:**
        - Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV every 4–6 hours PRN (slow IV injection over 5 minutes)
        - Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS (max 4 g/day)
    - **Antiemetic:**
        - Ondansetron 4 mg IV/IM/PO TDS (slow IV bolus)
        - Cyclizine 50 mg IM/PO TDS (alternative)
    - **Antibiotics (if signs of infection/rupture):**
        - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS **OR** cefuroxime 750 mg IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS (follow local antibiogram)
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Tramadol 50–100 mg PO/IV QDS (caution: seizure threshold)
    - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (if no contraindications; avoid if bleeding)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Morphine: avoid in respiratory depression (RR <10), caution in renal failure
    - Ondansetron: avoid if QTc >500 ms (check ECG if known long QT)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Morphine: reduce dose/frequency if eGFR <30; avoid if cirrhotic (hepatic encephalopathy)
    - Enoxaparin: reduce to 20 mg SC once daily if CrCl <30
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Morphine:** Never give IV without first checking respiratory rate and ensuring naloxone available. Overdose = respiratory arrest.
    - **IV ondansetron:** Ensure dilution + slow push; rapid injection can cause fatal arrhythmia.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - **Ovarian necrosis / infarction** – loss of ovary, risk of peritonitis and sepsis
    - **Rupture of necrotic ovary** – causes haemoperitoneum and peritonitis
    - **Septic shock** from pelvic infection (rare if not surgically treated)
    - **Ileus** post-laparoscopy/laparotomy
- **Chronic:**
    - **Pelvic adhesions** → chronic pain, reduced fertility
    - **Recurrence** of torsion (if contralateral ovary also enlarged)
    - **Ovarian failure** if both ovaries compromised (rare; only one twisted usually)
sections.Prognosis: - Ovarian salvage rate >90% if surgery **within 6 hours** of symptom onset; decreases to <50% after 24 hours. Even a black/burgundy ovary can recover function after detorsion. Most patients retain normal fertility with one ovary.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check a pregnancy test** before calling the gynaecology registrar – it’s the single most critical discriminator.
- **Document time of pain onset** explicitly in notes (medicolegal and prognostic key).
- **If you suspect torsion but US shows some flow, do NOT reassure** – intermittent torsion is real; call gynae. Absent flow is highly specific, present flow does not rule out.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay surgical review** for imaging if clinical presentation is classic (young woman, acute unilateral pain, vomiting, no alternative diagnosis). Time is ovary.
- **Do not give strong analgesia without securing a senior plan** – may mask progression or peritonism.
- **Do not omit VTE prophylaxis** – pelvic surgery carries moderate risk. Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily is standard (unless high bleed risk).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
*Note: Most patients with confirmed torsion will undergo surgery and be admitted. This section applies if discharged post-surgery or if torsion has been ruled out (e.g., resolved ovarian cyst, no torsion).*
- **Return if:**
    - Severe pain not controlled by oral analgesia
    - Fever >38.5°C, chills, or vomiting
    - Inability to pass urine or significant bleeding per vagina
- **Follow-up:**
    - Outpatient gynaecology clinic in 2 weeks (histology results, discuss recurrence risk)
    - If childbearing potential, advise to present early (within 1 hour) for any future acute pelvic pain
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call gynaecology registrar urgently:**
    - Any reproductive-age female with acute unilateral pelvic pain + vomiting + no alternative cause
    - NEWS2 ≥5 (tachycardia, hypotension, low oxygen saturations) – may indicate ruptured ectopic or septic shock
    - **Lactate >2.5** (high suspicion of infarction)
    - GCS drop <15 or signs of peritonism (rigid abdomen, rebound)
- **Crash team / anaesthetics:**
    - Hypotension unresponsive to 2 L fluid bolus, or any cardiac arrest rhythm
- **ICU referral:**
    - If post-op develops septic shock (lactate >4, vasopressor requirement) or multi-organ failure

rawMarkdown:
## Ovarian Torsion
**One-liner:** Gynaecological emergency where ovary twists on pedicle causing ischaemia; rapid diagnosis (hours) and surgery needed to preserve ovary and fertility.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** — *Finding: positive B-hCG + haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension, peritonism).* Haemorrhagic shock kills faster than torsion.
- **Tubo-ovarian abscess / septic shock** — *Finding: high fever (>38.5°C) + purulent discharge + cervical motion tenderness + leukocytosis (WCC >15).* Sepsis can mimic torsion but needs antibiotics > surgery initially.
- **Ovarian torsion with complete infarction and peritonitis** — *Finding: diffuse guarding/rigidity + rising lactate (>2.5) + NEWS2 ≥5 with no improvement.* Can progress to multi-organ failure if surgery delayed.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms** (3–5 things you’ll actually see):
    - Sudden-onset severe unilateral **lower abdominal / pelvic pain** (often colicky, may wax/ wane if intermittent torsion)
    - Pain **aggravated by movement** (coughing, walking)
    - **Nausea and/or vomiting** (common, can be prominent)
    - **Dysuria** or urinary frequency (due to bladder irritation)
    - **Fever** (late sign, suggests necrosis or superinfection)
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Unilateral **adnexal tenderness** on bimanual exam (bimanual is essential)
    - Lower abdominal tenderness ± **guarding/rebound** (peritoneal signs indicate advanced ischaemia)
    - Palpable **adnexal mass** (if >5 cm; often cystic or solid)
    - **Cervical motion tenderness** (non-specific)
    - Vital signs: tachycardia (early peritonism), hypotension (late, suspect rupture or septic shock)

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely → less likely with 1-line differentiators:
    - **Ectopic pregnancy:** positive B-hCG + free fluid on bedside US + cervical motion tenderness
    - **Ruptured ovarian cyst:** sudden pain but often with **transient hypotension** and free fluid; ultrasound shows collapsed cyst
    - **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID):** bilateral pain, vaginal discharge, fever, raised inflammatory markers; **cervical motion tenderness** on bimanual
    - **Appendicitis:** pain starts periumbilical then localises to RIF, anorexia, vomiting; **McBurney’s sign**, not adnexal tenderness
    - **Renal colic:** flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, costovertebral angle tenderness
    - **Diverticulitis:** left lower quadrant pain, fever, diarrhoea or constipation; **left-sided tenderness**, no adnexal mass
    - **Mesenteric adenitis:** right-sided pain + fever, but **lymphadenopathy** on US, and often normal gynaecological exam
- **Splitter:** *Positive pregnancy test + unilateral adnexal pain = consider ectopic first, not torsion. Order B-hCG stat.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
    - Urine **pregnancy test** (mandatory in any reproductive-age woman with lower abdominal pain)
    - **Vital signs** (tachycardia → hypovolaemia from rupture? hypotension → catastrophic)
    - **Urine dip** (rule out UTI/renal colic)
- **Bloods:**
    - **FBC** (WCC rise – infection/infarction; Hb drop – haemorrhage)
    - **CRP** (high in PID, infection, late torsion)
    - **Beta-hCG** (serum if urine positive or uncertain)
    - **U&Es, LFTs** (pre-operative baseline)
    - **Lactate** (elevated if ischaemic, can help confirm urgency)
    - **Group & save** (prepare for possible blood products)
    - **Coagulation screen** (pre-operative)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** **Transvaginal ultrasound with colour Doppler** (urgent, if available) – look for:
        - Enlarged ovary (≥4 cm), hyperechoic stroma, **whirlpool sign** of twisted vascular pedicle
        - **Absent or reduced arterial flow** on Doppler (not 100% sensitive; intermittent torsion may show flow)
        - Free fluid in pouch of Douglas (suggests rupture or ischaemia)
    - **Second-line:** **MRI abdomen** if US equivocal and still high suspicion (shows associated adnexal mass, twisted pedicle) – but **do not wait for MRI if classical presentation**
- **Special tests:** None routine; **diagnostic laparoscopy** is both gold standard and definitive treatment
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine B-hCG** – omission = potential missed ectopic with catastrophic haemorrhage

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **Stabilise** – IV access (two wide-bore cannulae if shock), fluid bolus (Hartmann’s 500 mL stat, repeat as needed)
    - **Analgesia** – morphine 2.5–5 mg IV every 4–6 hours PRN (with antiemetic)
    - **Antiemetic** – ondansetron 4 mg IV slow bolus
    - **NBM** (NBM from time of suspicion – for potential urgent laparoscopy)
    - **Urgent gynaecology referral** (on-call registrar; discuss within 15–30 minutes)
    - **Pregnancy test result** – if positive, urgent gynaecology + consider ectopic pathway
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - **Pre-operative:** monitor vital signs (NEWS2), repeat lactate if deteriorating; keep NBM; IV fluids at maintenance (Hartmann’s 1 L/8h [check local policy])
    - **Post-operative:**
        - Oral intake commenced once awake (usually day 1)
        - Analgesia (paracetamol 1 g QDS, tramadol [check local] or morphine PRN)
        - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily if BMI <30, higher if obese – but not if high bleeding risk)
        - Wound care – early mobilisation, encourage regular observations for signs of infection
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - If **ovarian cyst** detected (common cause), arrange outpatient ultrasound follow-up in 6 weeks to monitor cyst resolution
    - **Fertility follow-up:** if only one ovary preserved, reassure re fertility but discuss future pregnancy risks (adhesions, torsion of contralateral ovary)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **IV fluids:** Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline at 1 mL/kg/h (adjusted for NBM)
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if not high bleed risk)
    - **Diet:** NBM until seen by anaesthetist/gynaecologist (if pre-op); clear fluids post-op if tolerating
    - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly, escalate if ≥5

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Analgesia:**
        - Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV every 4–6 hours PRN (slow IV injection over 5 minutes)
        - Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS (max 4 g/day)
    - **Antiemetic:**
        - Ondansetron 4 mg IV/IM/PO TDS (slow IV bolus)
        - Cyclizine 50 mg IM/PO TDS (alternative)
    - **Antibiotics (if signs of infection/rupture):**
        - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS **OR** cefuroxime 750 mg IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS (follow local antibiogram)
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Tramadol 50–100 mg PO/IV QDS (caution: seizure threshold)
    - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (if no contraindications; avoid if bleeding)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Morphine: avoid in respiratory depression (RR <10), caution in renal failure
    - Ondansetron: avoid if QTc >500 ms (check ECG if known long QT)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Morphine: reduce dose/frequency if eGFR <30; avoid if cirrhotic (hepatic encephalopathy)
    - Enoxaparin: reduce to 20 mg SC once daily if CrCl <30
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Morphine:** Never give IV without first checking respiratory rate and ensuring naloxone available. Overdose = respiratory arrest.
    - **IV ondansetron:** Ensure dilution + slow push; rapid injection can cause fatal arrhythmia.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - **Ovarian necrosis / infarction** – loss of ovary, risk of peritonitis and sepsis
    - **Rupture of necrotic ovary** – causes haemoperitoneum and peritonitis
    - **Septic shock** from pelvic infection (rare if not surgically treated)
    - **Ileus** post-laparoscopy/laparotomy
- **Chronic:**
    - **Pelvic adhesions** → chronic pain, reduced fertility
    - **Recurrence** of torsion (if contralateral ovary also enlarged)
    - **Ovarian failure** if both ovaries compromised (rare; only one twisted usually)

### Prognosis
- Ovarian salvage rate >90% if surgery **within 6 hours** of symptom onset; decreases to <50% after 24 hours. Even a black/burgundy ovary can recover function after detorsion. Most patients retain normal fertility with one ovary.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check a pregnancy test** before calling the gynaecology registrar – it’s the single most critical discriminator.
- **Document time of pain onset** explicitly in notes (medicolegal and prognostic key).
- **If you suspect torsion but US shows some flow, do NOT reassure** – intermittent torsion is real; call gynae. Absent flow is highly specific, present flow does not rule out.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay surgical review** for imaging if clinical presentation is classic (young woman, acute unilateral pain, vomiting, no alternative diagnosis). Time is ovary.
- **Do not give strong analgesia without securing a senior plan** – may mask progression or peritonism.
- **Do not omit VTE prophylaxis** – pelvic surgery carries moderate risk. Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily is standard (unless high bleed risk).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
*Note: Most patients with confirmed torsion will undergo surgery and be admitted. This section applies if discharged post-surgery or if torsion has been ruled out (e.g., resolved ovarian cyst, no torsion).*
- **Return if:**
    - Severe pain not controlled by oral analgesia
    - Fever >38.5°C, chills, or vomiting
    - Inability to pass urine or significant bleeding per vagina
- **Follow-up:**
    - Outpatient gynaecology clinic in 2 weeks (histology results, discuss recurrence risk)
    - If childbearing potential, advise to present early (within 1 hour) for any future acute pelvic pain

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call gynaecology registrar urgently:**
    - Any reproductive-age female with acute unilateral pelvic pain + vomiting + no alternative cause
    - NEWS2 ≥5 (tachycardia, hypotension, low oxygen saturations) – may indicate ruptured ectopic or septic shock
    - **Lactate >2.5** (high suspicion of infarction)
    - GCS drop <15 or signs of peritonism (rigid abdomen, rebound)
- **Crash team / anaesthetics:**
    - Hypotension unresponsive to 2 L fluid bolus, or any cardiac arrest rhythm
- **ICU referral:**
    - If post-op develops septic shock (lactate >4, vasopressor requirement) or multi-organ failure
